coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-rich soybean protein isolate derived “Self-Doping” carbon nano-onions for luminescence properties
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Yunhao
Guo, Dandan
Wang, Yuang
Gao, Ying
Yang, Longyuan
Lyu, Yinong
Xu, Hui
Ran, Shuai
Li, Na
Zhang, Xitong
Pan, Yueshen
Yang, Zixu
Zhu, Lin
Zhu, Yuezhao
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nano-onions (CNOs) were successfully obtained for the first time through a simple one-pot Microwave hydrothermal carbonization (MHTC) method at low temperature by using biomass macromolecules as the raw materials. The effects of different reaction temperatures and residence times on the characteristics and yield of CNOs were studied. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images reveal CNOs with a regular concentric carbon shell structure with 10–20 layers, obvious crystal characteristics, and the lattice spacing of 0.34 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and photoluminescence spectroscopy were applied to reveal the effects of different nitrogen forms and hybrid structures of carbon on the fluorescence properties of CNOs. Results showed that reaction temperature affected the structure of CNOs mainly by regulating the degree of carbonization, and residence time mainly affected the aromatization of CNOs. In addition, nitrogen self-doping CNOs prepared at low temperature also have good fluorescence performance. Most strikingly, the stable blue light emission by nitrogen self-doping CNOs could be the combination of the fluorescence of the carbon shell sp2 structure and that of surface functional groups. Taking advantage of the above unique fluorescent behavior, CNOs are prepared as fluorescent anticounterfeiting printing to achieve data encryption and decryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nano-onions
Microwave hydrothermal carbonization
Nitrogen self-doping
Luminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of reaction temperature and residence time on product yields

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental flowchart of soy protein isolate MHTC preparation of S-CNOs.
Effects of reaction temperature and residence time on product yields of S-CNOs.
SEM images of S-CNOs at different reaction temperatures.
(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of S-CNOs.
XRD pattern of S-CNOs at different (a) reaction temperatures and (b) residence times.
FTIR spectra of S-CNOs at different (a) reaction temperatures and (b) residence times.
(a) XPS full-scan survey, (b) high-resolution XPS of C1s, (c) high-resolution XPS of N1s and (d) high-resolution XPS of O1s of S-CNOs under different working conditions.

S-CNOs supernatant and ultraviolet luminescence images prepared under different conditions at various dilutions (a) Undiluted solution; (b) Dilute to 5 times; (c) Dilute to 10 times; (d) Dilute to colorless.
UV–Vis spectra of S-CNOs at different (a) reaction temperatures and (b) residence times.
Excitation–emission spectra of S-CNOs at different (a) reaction temperatures and (b) residence times.
The images of fluorescent inks with different pH under (a) natural light and (b) 365 nm UV light and the fluorescent seal under (c) natural light and (d) 365 nm UV light.
The relative contents of C, N, O and some of their different chemical states in S-CNOs prepared under different working conditions.
The absolute quantum yield of S-CNOs prepared under different working conditions.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-rich soybean protein isolate derived “Self-Doping” carbon nano-onions for luminescence properties

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nano-onions (CNOs) were successfully obtained for the first time through a simple one-pot Microwave hydrothermal carbonization (MHTC) method at low temperature by using biomass macromolecules as the raw materials. The effects of different reaction temperatures and residence times on the characteristics and yield of CNOs were studied. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images reveal CNOs with a regular concentric carbon shell structure with 10–20 layers, obvious crystal characteristics, and the lattice spacing of 0.34 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and photoluminescence spectroscopy were applied to reveal the effects of different nitrogen forms and hybrid structures of carbon on the fluorescence properties of CNOs. Results showed that reaction temperature affected the structure of CNOs mainly by regulating the degree of carbonization, and residence time mainly affected the aromatization of CNOs. In addition, nitrogen self-doping CNOs prepared at low temperature also have good fluorescence performance. Most strikingly, the stable blue light emission by nitrogen self-doping CNOs could be the combination of the fluorescence of the carbon shell sp2 structure and that of surface functional groups. Taking advantage of the above unique fluorescent behavior, CNOs are prepared as fluorescent anticounterfeiting printing to achieve data encryption and decryption.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomass is an important renewable resource worldwide. It is the fourth largest energy source after oil, coal, and natural gas and has become an important material basis for sustainable development [1]. The production of a variety of high-quality end products from waste biomass is an important approach to the large-scale and resource-based utilization of renewable energy. The controllable directional preparation and performance study of high-quality biomass-based carbon nanomaterials are the key and difficult points in the efficient conversion of biomass. In addition to traditional carbon materials, such as graphene nanosheets, carbon nanotubes, and carbon nanospheres, carbon dots (CDs) and carbon nano-onions (CNOs) are two emerging biomass-based fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with extremely broad application prospects.

CDs generally refer to carbon nanomaterials with a particle size of less than 20 nm that are formed after complete carbonization; they are composed of a carbon core and various surface functional groups[2]. Since CDs were accidentally discovered by Xu et al.[3] in 2004, they have attracted increasing attention due to their excellent fluorescence performance and wide application prospects. The fluorescence mechanism analysis of CDs can be divided into the following four categories [2,4,5]: carbon core luminescence, surface state luminescence, molecular fluorescence, and cross-linked enhanced emission effect.

CNOs, another type of emerging carbon nanomaterials, are composed of several layers of concentric spherical graphite shells of large carbon atom clusters and have sizes of approximately 2–100 nm [6]. Given that they not only have high specific surface areas [7–10], electrical conductivity [10], thermal stability [11], and chemical stability but also a closed and stable structure [12], they have good application prospects in energy, catalysis, lubrication, and numerous other fields. In previous studies, the methods for the synthesis of CNOs mainly included electron beam irradiation [13], arc discharge [14], chemical vapor deposition [15], and pyrolysis [16]. These synthesis methods require extremely strict conditions and high temperatures. A recent study found that CNOs and CDs can be converted into each other by changing experimental variables. By using graphene carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as intermediates, Song et al. [17] synthesized CNOs with concentric fullerene-like carbon shells through hydrothermal carbonization with citric acid at 180 °C. Liu et al. [18] synthesized ultraviolet- and blue-light-emitting CQDs through the chemical oxidation of CNOs. Gaber et al. [19] carbonized tomatoes at 240 °C to produce CNOs and obtained only CDs in the absence of the catalyst. Ari et al. [20] utilized homogenized cellulose powder as the raw material and, by extending treatment time, obtained CDs and CNOs through the mechanical–chemical method at high temperatures. Some of the CNOs obtained via these methods have unique fluorescence characteristics. Although the luminescence mechanism is not clear, it also provides a new idea to us. The fluorescence phenomena of CDs and CNOs prepared through the same method may have the same explanation. Therefore, in consideration of the luminescence mechanism of CDs, we believe that the fluorescence effect of CNOs is mainly the combination of the luminescence of the carbon shell sp2 structure and that of surface functional groups.

The main factors affecting the synthesis of CNOs include preparation methods, reaction materials, reaction temperature, residence time, and doping. Our previous studies have also shown that temperature, time and the nonthermal effect of microwaves can affect the fluorescence effect of carbon nanomaterials [21–23]. Therefore, we chose microwave hydrothermal carbonization (MHTC) as the method for CNOs preparation. This method has the advantages of the hydrothermal and microwave methods. Nanodiamond [24,25] and simple compounds [26–28] are commonly chosen as the raw materials for CNOs synthesis. However, few studies have been done on biomass model compounds. Reaction temperatures and residence times also have great influence on the properties of CNOs. In this work, the effects of reaction temperatures and residence time son the formation of protein-based CNOs were studied, and their effects on fluorescence characteristics were explored.

In the best of our knowledge, there is no exact report about the preparation and fluorescence properties of CNOs by simple biomass model compound with nitrogen self-doping. Therefore, the study of reaction temperature and residence time in MHTC on the morphology and fluorescence characteristics of protein-based CNOs is novel and unique, which lays a foundation for the utilization of nitrogen-rich biomass. The results indicated that the fluorescence mechanism of N self-doping CNOs was ascribed to the combination of the fluorescence of the carbon shell sp2 structure and that of surface functional groups. The low-cost and eco-friendly preparation and favorable fluorescence properties of CNOs are believed to provide an alternative synthesis route into multicolour CNOs-based materials, and make the CNOs promising for further applications, such as fluorescent anti-counterfeiting and light-emitting diodes.

Soy protein isolate (SPI) from Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. was used as the protein in this experiment. It was a dispersed reagent in the form of a light yellow powder. Deionized water (DW) was provided by a smart-Q deionized pure water machine. The reagents used (glycerin, HCl, NaOH) were analytically pure and came from Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD.

The SPI-based CNOs were synthesized via MHTC. The preparation process is illustrated in Fig. 1 . A total of 2 g of SPI was transferred into a 100 ml Teflon™-lined reaction vessel and fully mixed with 50 ml of DW. A magnetic stir bar was placed in the reaction vessel, and the vessel was then sealed and placed in a microwave workstation (maximum power: 1000 W; XO-SM100; Nanjing Xianou, China). The microwave power and magnetic stirring speed were set as 500 W and 200 rpm, respectively. Then, the materials were heated in accordance with different reaction conditions as shown in Table 1 . After the reaction had occurred, the reaction vessel was cooled to room temperature. The suspension was then ultracentrifuged at 10 000 rpm for 3 min to remove large carbon particles, and the solid residue was separated and dried for yield analysis. Centrifugation was repeated twice. The supernatant solution containing the SPI-based CNOs was collected and dialyzed against DW by using a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da) for 48 h. The DW used for dialysis was changed every 4 h. After dialysis, the SPI-based CNOs suspension was obtained. A portion of the samples was saved, and another portion was freeze-dried with a lyophilizer for 48 h for further characterization.

At least three repeated experiments were performed for each group of experiments. The ratio of the solid phase and the gas–liquid biphase in the three-phase product was quantified in accordance with the average value recorded in repeated experiments. Given that MHTC is a low-temperature hydrothermal carbonization process, the yield of the gas phase can be almost neglected and can be counted together with the yield of the liquid phase. The formulas for the yields of the solid phase and gas–liquid phase are represented by (1) and (2).
(1)
Y s
=
m s
/ m × 100 %
(2)
Y 1
= 100 % -
Y s
In (1) and (2), Ys is the solid phase yield, ms is the solid phase quality, m is the raw material quality, and Yl is the liquid–gas phase yield.
For clarity and simplicity, the CNOs prepared with SPI as raw materials are abbreviated as S-CNOs (SPI-based CNOs).
And the fluorescent ink was made of 20 mg S-CNOs powder dissolved in 4 ml of DW, plus 1 ml of glycerol.

S-CNOs were mostly nanoparticles with sizes of less than 20 nm, and their morphology and microstructure were recorded by using a high-resolution electron transmission microscope (HRTEM), model JEM-2100F (Japan). A JSM-7600F (Japan) field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) was used to characterize the surface morphology of S-CNOs with large particle sizes. The structural properties of S-CNOs were characterized on the basis of X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra. The X-ray diffractometer model was Miniflex 600 (Japan) with a Cu target (λ = 0.154 nm) and the 2θ range of 5°–70°, scanning speed of 8°/min, tube voltage of 40 kV, and tube current of 30 mA. The surface functionalization of the S-CNOs was characterized via Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy experiments on a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer with KBr pellets. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a Thermo ESCALAB 250 instrument by using monochromatic Al Kα radiation; the building energy step size was set as 0.100 eV. The absorption spectrum of S-CNOs was acquired with a Perkin Elmer Lambda 750 s (U.S.) dual-beam, dual-monochromator UV–Vis spectrophotometer, and the excitation wavelength range was 200–500 nm. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of S-CNOs were recorded with a Perkin Elmer FL6500 (USA) fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 280–480 nm and excitation every 20 nm. The absolute quantum yield was measured by using Hamamatsu C11347-11 (Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of reaction temperature and residence time on product yields
ce:section.ce:para: Product distribution under different working conditions is shown in Fig. 2 . As can be seen in this figure, the increase in reaction temperature significantly increases the liquid–gas yield of SPI. At the reaction temperature of 160 °C, the liquid–gas yield is 76%, and the solid-phase yield is 24%. When the reaction temperature is increased to 210 °C, the liquid–gas yield increases to 94%, and the solid phase yield decreases to 6%. Residence time has little effect on the liquid–gas yield of SPI. When the residence time is increased from 30 min to 180 min, the liquid–gas yield increases by 6% from 89% to 95%. The overall high liquid-gas yield indicates that SPI can realize the decomposition of raw materials at a low temperature and a short time, so as to obtain nanoscale water-soluble S-CNOs. The increase in reaction temperature and residence time can effectively promote the hydrolysis of SPI, thereby increasing the liquid–gas yield. In this process, the effect of the reaction temperature is more significant than that of residence time. When the temperature is higher than 200 °C or the residence time is longer than 60 min, the liquid phase yield exceeds 90%, and the reaction is considered to be relatively complete at this time. Therefore, the temperature of 200 °C and the time of 60 min can be considered as the best conditions for research.

Given that reaction temperature has a greater influence on the formation of S-CNOs than residence time, S-CNOs synthesized at different temperatures were characterized by using SEM. The results are shown in Fig. 3 . The reaction of SPI over the low temperature range of 160 °C–180 °C is insufficient, and the solid sample of S-CNOs is in a state of fragmentation. Large fragments with a certain degree of looseness are found when the reaction temperature is 160 °C. A small number of spherical particles appear at 170 °C, and a large number of small fragments with the highest degree of fragmentation appear at 180 °C. As the reaction temperature is further increased, the S-CNOs present a gradually decreasing fragmentation degree and a loose planar structure. The obvious crystal defects observed in the S-CNOs samples fabricated at 190 °C–200 °C indicate the formation of nanoscale structures. When the temperature is increased to 210 °C, almost no fragments can be seen mainly because the carbonization degree of S-CNOs has gradually increased with the increase in reaction temperature.

Fig. 4 shows the TEM and HRTEM images of S-CNOs obtained at 200 °C with the residence time of 60 min. Fig. 4(a) shows that S-CNOs present general dispersibility in the supernatant with a small amount of areas with agglomeration. S-CNOs are relatively uniform in size with particle sizes between 7 and 13 nm. As illustrated in Fig. 4(b), S-CNOs exhibit a regular concentric carbon shell structure with 10–20 layers and obvious crystal characteristics. Random tests on the lattice size show that the lattice spacing is 0.34 nm, which corresponds to the (002) crystal plane of graphite [29,30]. In this work, CNOs were obtained for the first time via a simple MHTC one-pot method at low temperature by using a biomass model compound as the raw materials.

The crystal structure of S-CNOs was analyzed on the basis of XRD patterns as illustrated in Fig. 5 . The S-CNOs prepared at different reaction temperatures and reaction times have uniform characteristic peaks with 2θ values ranging from 20° to 23°. These diffraction peaks are attributed to the graphite (002) crystal plane with the interplanar spacing of 0.34 nm. This result indicates that the sp2-hybridized conjugated region is an important component of S-CNOs and is also in good agreement with the HRTEM results. The broad peak corresponds to the disordered carbon structure, which is manifested by small particle size, wide particle size distribution, and defects in the crystal structure. The significant enhancement in peak strength and peak width with the increase in reaction temperature and residence time indicates that the carbonization degree of the product has increased, the crystal defects have been enhanced, and nanosized S-CNOs of different sizes have formed further. These results are consistent with the SEM results. In addition, with the increase in temperature and time, obvious small peaks that are typical characteristics of fullerene are found at 2θ = 20°, 23°, and 31°. The presence of these peaks show that fullerene fragments have formed during the formation of S-CNOs.

The functional characteristics of S-CNOs prepared under different working conditions were analyzed by FTIR as illustrated in Fig. 6 . The S-CNOs in the figure show an absorption peak at 3270 cm−1 that represents the stretching vibration of the –NH bond, indicating the existence of nitrogenous functional groups in S-CNOs. The characteristic absorption peak at 1454 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum is attributed to C–N vibration in the amide III band, and the absorption peak at 1292 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of C–N, further proving that N has successfully self-doping into S-CNOs. Amide I (1640 cm−1) and amide II (1520 cm−1) are two typical protein peaks [31]. In addition, the absorption peaks at 2923–2964 and 1398–1400 cm−1 in the spectrum correspond to the stretching vibration of the C–H bond and the deformation vibration of the C–H bond, respectively. The absorption peaks at 1032–1102 and 880 cm−1 in the fingerprint region correspond to the stretching vibration of the C–O bond and the stretching vibration of the C–C bond [32–35]. The gradual shift of amide I to C=O and the gradual weakening of amide II with the increase in temperature indicate that the protein structure of SPI is destroyed during the synthesis of S-CNOs via MHTC. A weak peak is still observed at 210 °C, indicating that the hydrolysis and carbonization of protein are incomplete. C–N stretching vibration appears from 180 °C, and this characteristic peak gradually strengthens with the increase in temperature over the range of 180 °C–210 °C. Moreover, the C–N stretching vibration of the amide band III at 210 °C is also slightly greater than that at 180 °C. Both results show that the self-doping of N becomes increasingly favorable as the temperature is increased. C=C vibration at 1594 cm−1 can be observed when the temperature is elevated to 190 °C. When the temperature is further increased to 210 °C, the peak becomes increasingly defined and strengthens. This spectral change implies that a greater degree of aromatization occurs at this time than at other times. The gradual increment in the C–H stretching vibration at 2964 cm−1 and the C–H deformation vibration at 1400 cm−1 of the spectra with the increase in temperature shows that the secondary cleavage of the hydrolysates of proteins promote chain-opening and ring-opening reactions, making the spectrum display a certain degree of aliphatic and alkane functional characteristics. Residence time has less influence on functional characteristics than reaction temperature. With the increase in residence time, the intensity of the C–O tensile vibration at 1070 cm−1 and the C=C vibration characteristic peak at 1594 cm−1 gradually increases, indicating that additional oxygen-containing functional groups have been introduced into S-CNOs, hydrophilicity has been enhanced, and the aromatization degree of S-CNOs has increased. As can be seen from the FTIR results, reaction temperature has a great effect on the structure of S-CNOs, and the elevation of temperature is conducive to the doping of N and the carbonization of S-CNOs. Consistent with the results of the previous structural characterization of S-CNOs, the formation of C=C after 190 °C indicates that the multilayered sp2 hybridization fullerene shell structure of CNOs has gradually formed.

Table 2 shows the relative contents of C, N, O, and some of their different chemical states in S-CNOs prepared under different working conditions. Fig. 7 provides the XPS spectra of S-CNOs produced under four working conditions. Fig. 7(a) shows the full XPS spectra of S-CNOs fabricated under different conditions. N (N1s, 398.9 eV) exists in addition to C (C1s, 284.9 eV) and O (O1s, 530.9 eV). Table 2 illustrates that the obtained N doping ratio is close to 14%. This result indicates that good N self-doping has been obtained without the addition of any exogenous nitrogen source. In addition, the increase in reaction temperature and residence time affects the increase in C content mainly via the strengthening of the degree of carbonization. Fig. 7(b) shows that the C1s spectrum of S-CNOs has five main peaks at 284.5, 285, 286, 287.3, and 288.1 eV that correspond to C=C (sp2), C–C (sp3), C–N, C–O, and C=O, respectively [29,36]. This figure shows that the ratio of C–C (sp3) and C=O increases with the elevation of reaction temperature mainly due to the further hydrolysis and carbonization of SPI and the increase in oxygen-containing functional groups in S-CNOs. The prolongation of residence time has a highly significant effect on the increase in C=C (sp2) ratio, indicating that prolonged reaction times can strengthen the aromatization of S–CNOs and result in the formation of sp2 hybrid carbon structures. This result is in good agreement with the results of the FTIR spectra. As presented in Fig. 7(c), the N1s spectra of S-CNOs are fitted into four peak positions that each represent four chemical forms of N-doping in S-CNOs: pyridinic N (398.5 e V), amine N (399.7 e V), pyrrolic N (400.2 e V), and quaternary N (401.3 e V) [37]. As can be seen from the data in Table 2, the pyridinic N content in S-CNOs always remains at a very low level of less than 5%. The increase in reaction temperature and residence time can promote the conversion of amine N into quaternary N. In addition, the extension of residence time can further promote the transformation of pyrrolic N into quaternary N. As depicted in Fig. 7(d), the O1s spectra of S-CNOs are fitted with two peaks at 531.3 and 532.4 eV representing two different chemical states of O, C=O, and C–OH/C–O–C. The increase in reaction temperature and that in residence time have the same effect: promoting the conversion of C–OH/C–O–C into C=O. Combining the FTIR and XPS results reveals that the increase in residence time mainly affects the aromatization degree of S-CNOs, whereas the sp2 C structure can reduce the band gap and dominate the fluorescence of the carbon shell structure. The elevation of reaction temperature can promote the decarboxylation and deamination of amino acids and form active functional groups, such as C-N, C–O, and C=O. At the same time, given the nucleophilic action of the electron on the arc of nitrogen, S-CNOs undergo self-polymerization, thus completing the self-doping of nitrogen and yielding four doped nitrogen states. These active functional groups and different doped nitrogen states are closely related to surface luminescence. Therefore, reaction temperatures and residence times have important effects on the fluorescence of S-CNOs.

Fig. 8 shows the supernatants and UV luminescence images of S-CNOs fabricated under different conditions and at various dilutions. As can be seen from Fig. 8(a), as the reaction temperature is increased, the color of the S-CNOs supernatant gradually darkens and appears deepest at 190 °C. Moreover, the luminescence of the undiluted stock solution under 365 nm ultraviolet light shows a certain filtration effect, weak blue-green fluorescence, and a barely perceptible fluorescence effect. The diluted S-CNOs supernatant and ultraviolet luminescence images are shown in Fig. 8(b)–(d). These S-CNOs supernatants exhibit a significantly weakened internal filtration effect, strengthened fluorescence luminescence, and a bright blue-green glow. The S-CNOs supernatant that has been further diluted into a nearly colorless solution still present obvious fluorescent luminescence effects. As can be easily seen, the fluorescence is improved with the increase in temperature, and the light emission is better over the range of 180 °C–210 °C. The residence time has a smaller effect on the luminescence of S-CNOs than the reaction temperature. The S-CNOs prepared at 200 °C with different residence times show slight differences in their luminescence intensities, and all display bright blue-green fluorescence.

Table 3 shows the absolute quantum yields of S-CNOs prepared under different working conditions. The absolute quantum yields were obtained with the excitation wavelength of 340 nm and the scanning range of emission wavelength of 320–650 nm. The reaction temperature and residence time are positively correlated with the absolute quantum yield of S-CNOs via different influencing mechanisms. The carbonization degree of S-CNOs is mainly affected by the reaction temperature, whereas the aromatization degree of S-CNOs is mainly affected by the residence time. In addition, the absolute quantum yield is consistent with the change rule of the product liquid phase yield, indicating that a high liquid-phase yield is beneficial to the production of S-CNOs.

As shown in Fig. 9 , the UV/Vis absorption characteristics of S-CNOs prepared under different working conditions were compared after normalization. The absorption spectra of S-CNOs produced under different working conditions have three uniform absorption bands, with the first, second, and third bands located at approximately 230, 270, and 325 nm. The absorption peaks at 235 and 270 nm correspond to the π–π* transitions of the C=C bonds in S-CNOs shells. The absorption peak at 325 nm corresponds to C=O or the n– π* transition with the N chromaticity group. Fig. 9(a) shows that the first absorption peak of S-CNOs at the reaction temperature of 160 °C is found at 222 nm. When the temperature is gradually increased to 210 °C, the absorption peak is red-shifted to 232 nm. The changes in the chemical structure of S-CNOs caused by the increment in reaction temperature are reflected in the absorption characteristics. These changes, in combination with the FTIR spectrum, suggest that the decrement in band gap is mainly caused by the gradual appearance of C=C and C–N functional groups at 170 °C that causes the absorption spectral band of S-CNOs to move in the direction of long wavelengths and produce a certain red shift. As can also be seen from the spectrum, the second and third absorption peaks at 268 and 325 nm presented by the S-CNOs produced at 210 °C are significantly stronger than those shown by the S-CNOs synthesized at 160 °C. The XPS analysis results indicate that the relative C=O, pyrrolic N, and quaternary N contents of S-CNOs obtained at 210 °C are all higher than those of S-CNOs obtained at 160 °C. This result implies that the enhancement of these two absorption peaks may be caused by C=O or the n–π* transition of the N chromaticity group. Moreover, consistent with the increase in N self-doping ratio, the peak intensity of the two absorption peaks gradually increases with the elevation of temperature. As can be seen from Fig. 9(b), residence time has a negligible effect on the UV/Vis absorption characteristics of S-CNOs. After 60 min, the absorption curves basically coincide, indicating that reaction at 200 °C for 60 min is a relatively appropriate and economical reaction condition.

Fig. 10 shows the excitation–emission spectra of S-CNOs prepared at different reaction temperatures and residence times. The spectra were acquired with the excitation wavelengths of 280–510 nm and excitation every 10 nm. With the increase in the reaction temperature, the fluorescence intensity of S-CNOs increases, and the luminescence center shifts at the same time. In the low temperature range of 160 °C–170 °C, the best excitation wavelength is 287 nm, and the maximum PL emission peak is concentrated near 360 nm. When the excitation wavelength is 330 nm, the S-CNOs have a secondary emission center, and the PL emission peak is concentrated near 420 nm. At this time, the optimal excitation wavelength of 287 nm corresponds to the transition of nN 2p → σ* [38], which is caused by the amino groups on the surfaces of S-CNOs. It can be seen that N self-doping S-CNOs prepared at low temperature also shows good fluorescence performance due to nitrogen-containing functional groups. The excitation wavelength at 330 nm corresponds to the π–π* transition in the aromatic sp2 domain [39]. This result is consistent with FTIR results. When the reaction temperature is further increased, the PL emission peak at 360 nm gradually disappears, and the PL emission peak at 420 nm gradually increases and redshifts to 440 nm. Furthermore, the corresponding optimal excitation wavelength is 360 nm. The gradual disappearance of the PL emission peak at 360 nm is due to the gradual decrease in amino groups on the surface of S-CNOs with the increase in temperature, and the PL emission peak at 420 nm is enhanced because the increase in temperature enhances the carbonization and aromatization of S-CNOs, thus strengthening the π–π* transition. The redshift of the PL emission peak from 420 nm to 440 nm is due to the formation of large conjugate structure and the increase in N doping ratio, thus reducing the band gap width of S-CNOs such that it can be excited by long wavelengths. The oblique light streaks on the spectrum are caused by Rayleigh scattering. They are almost invisible when the temperature is increased to 200 °C. The oblique development of the luminescence center indicates that the fluorescence of S-CNOs is excitation-dependent likely because different surface states can be excited by a specific range of wavelengths in accordance with energy distribution. For example, the C=O and C–O functional groups are excited at the wavelengths of 335 and 430 nm, respectively [40]. As the excitation wavelength is changed, the surface states are excited in turn, leading to a change in luminescence. Eventually, S-CNOs with a large number of functional groups may exhibit excitation wavelength-dependent characteristics. The effect of residence time on the emission spectra of S-CNOs is mainly reflected by the increase in fluorescence intensity. S-CNOs prepared with a residence time of 180 min have the strongest fluorescence intensity. Under this condition, the best excitation wavelength is 360 nm, and the PL emission peak is concentrated at approximately 420 nm. The results of FTIR and XPS show that residence time mainly affects the aromatization degree of S-CNOs. Similarly, Macairan et al. [41] demonstrated that the increase in residence time can significantly promote the growth of the sp2 network of the carbon shell. The enhancement in π–π* transition in the S-CNOs carbon core is the main reason for the increase in fluorescence intensity with residence time.

Finally, due to the excellent water solubility and fluorescence characteristics of the obtained S-CNOs, we used S-CNOs as a fluorescent ink for anticounterfeiting printing and tested their fluorescence stability. As shown in Fig. 11 , S-CNOs powder was used to configure fluorescent ink, its fluorescence stability with different pH was tested, and words were imprinted on paper without background fluorescence. In Fig. 11(a), the fluorescent inks with different pH all show light yellow under natural light, and there is no obvious difference, while the fluorescent inks show bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light in Fig. 11(b). Although the fluorescence is weakened in a strong acid or alkali environment, in general, fluorescent inks have a certain degree of fluorescence stability at various pH values. Under natural light, the engraved text is barely visible (Fig. 11c), whereas under 365 nm UV light, the text displays bright blue fluorescence (Fig. 11d). In addition, the absence of obvious fluorescence quenching under prolonged irradiation with strong ultraviolet light indicates that the prepared S-CNOs have good photobleaching stability. Therefore, the obtained S-CNOs can be used for anticounterfeiting printing or anticounterfeiting labels.

N self-doping S-CNOs with highly stable blue fluorescence were prepared successfully, and the effects of reaction temperature and residence time on morphology and fluorescence characteristics were investigated. In general, the size of the prepared S-CNOs varied from 7 nm to 13 nm. The S-CNOs exhibited a regular concentric carbon shell structure with 10–20 layers and obvious crystal characteristics with the lattice spacing of 0.34 nm. On the basis of this result, reaction temperature affected the structure of S-CNOs mainly by regulating the degree of carbonization, and the fluorescence of the surface functional groups of S-CNOs gradually changed into carbon shell fluorescence with the increase in reaction temperature. And the N self-doping S-CNOs prepared at low temperature also shows good fluorescence performance due to nitrogen-containing functional groups. Residence time mainly affected the aromatization of S-CNOs and enhanced carbon shell luminescence by promoting the growth of the carbon shell sp2 structure. Therefore, S-CNOs luminescence was the result of the interaction between the fluorescence of the carbon shell sp2 structure and that of the surface functional groups. Finally, the reaction temperature of 200 °C and the residence time of 60 min were found to be economical and applicable reaction conditions, and the S-CNOs prepared under these conditions could be used as fluorescent ink with good stability. Considering their excellent fluorescence properties, S-CNOs appear to be extraordinary carbon nanomaterials that are worthy of attention and have broad application prospects.

Yunhao Lin: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Dandan Guo: Validation, Writing – review & editing. Yuang Wang: Visualization, Formal analysis. Ying Gao: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Longyuan Yang: Resources, Formal analysis. Yinong Lyu: Validation, Resources. Hui Xu: Validation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources. Shuai Ran: Visualization. Na Li: Formal analysis. Xitong Zhang: Resources. Yueshen Pan: Resources. Zixu Yang: Resources. Lin Zhu: Visualization. Yuezhao Zhu: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20201365); Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Process Enhancement and New Energy Equipment Technology; the Top-notch Academic Program Project of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions; State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering (KL20-06); Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (19KJB610001).
